claim.statement: Phasic auditory alertness increases visual processing speed and lowers the threshold of conscious perception; the increase in processing speed grows with the intensity of the alerting cue.
claim.statement_location: Abstract, lines 7–13 ("Results show that phasic auditory alertness affects visual perception by increasing the visual processing speed and lowering the threshold of conscious perception… we further observed a positive relationship between alerting intensity and processing speed…")

data.source: Laboratory experiments with human participants performing single-letter recognition tasks
data.sample_size: Experiment 1: 28 participants; Experiment 2: 28 participants; Experiment 3: 25 participants (behavioural) / 24 participants (pupillometry)
data.notes: Participants were Danish university students with normal or corrected vision and hearing; tasks executed on 21" CRT monitor at 100 Hz; alerting cues delivered via loudspeakers.

method.description: Across three laboratory experiments, participants identified briefly presented, backward-masked letters that were sometimes preceded by an auditory alerting cue of varying intensity (0 dB, 40 dB, 85 dB). Behavioural accuracy across eight exposure durations was modelled with Bundesen’s Theory of Visual Attention to obtain estimates of visual processing speed (v) and perceptual threshold (t0).
steps: Recruit and screen participants for normal vision and hearing.
Present fixation cross during an inter-trial interval drawn from a non-aging distribution.
On cue trials, play a 200 ms auditory tone (500 Hz or 900 Hz) at the assigned intensity (40 dB or 85 dB); no tone on control trials.
After a cue–target onset asynchrony (200–470 ms, non-aging distribution), display a single target letter for one of eight exposure durations (10–80 ms) left or right of fixation.
Immediately mask the letter for 500 ms at both positions.
Collect unspeeded verbal or keypress report of the letter identity; give accuracy feedback.
Repeat for 960–1280 trials per participant depending on experiment.
Fit TVA model separately for each participant and condition using maximum-likelihood estimation (Nelder–Mead simplex in MATLAB) to extract v and t0.
Statistically compare parameters across alerting conditions with paired t-tests.
method.models: Bundesen’s Theory of Visual Attention (TVA) single-letter model with guessing parameter; maximum-likelihood estimation of v and t0; paired t-tests for condition comparisons.
method.outcome_variable: TVA-estimated visual processing speed (v) and perceptual threshold (t0)
method.independent_variables: Presence/absence of auditory cue; cue intensity (40 dB vs 85 dB)
method.control_variables: Exposure duration, letter identity, cue pitch (balanced high/low), target side, non-aging foreperiod distributions
method.tools_software: E-Prime 2 for stimulus presentation; MATLAB (Nelder–Mead simplex) for model fitting; Eyelink 1000 eye tracker for pupil data (Experiment 3)

results.summary: In Experiment 1, an 85 dB auditory cue significantly increased processing speed (t(27)=4.71, p<0.001) and lowered perceptual threshold (t(27)=3.52, p=0.002) relative to no-cue trials. Experiment 2 showed that processing speed rose monotonically with cue intensity (no-cue < 40 dB < 85 dB), while thresholds were equally reduced in both cue conditions. Experiment 3 replicated these behavioural patterns and demonstrated parallel increases in phasic pupil dilation, linking heightened alertness to faster visual processing.

metadata.original_paper_title: The effect of phasic auditory alerting on visual perception
